segments.text: So, hello. I'm John Clifton. I'm a product manager at Sonar. Writing code is easier and faster than ever. It's
writing good quality code that solves the problem and is ready for production. That's the real challenge.
And I'm very pleased to welcome Cole Medin to talk about exactly this. Cole is a tech leader and educator
passionate about helping users of AI agents and coding assistants to solve real business problems in a way that
truly scales. So let's get cracking. First of all, Cole, from your research, how big a problem is this?
Yeah, so that's a good question. So couple of numbers that I have here to really set the stage for how big of a
deal this is. First one is that Sonar did a survey at the start of this year. They found that 42% of committed code is
already AI generated. And by the way, that was only 6% in 2023. So back then
we had, you know, like tab complete with GitHub copilot and maybe you take a couple of code snippets from chat GBT
and bring that into your codebase. But now we have full-blown coding agents that can build entire features end to
end autonomously. And that number is projected to get to 65% by the end of
the year. But there's a really big disconnect that we have. So 96% of
developers, not the vibe coders of the world, but developers, they distrust AI
outputed code, but only 48% of them will actually validate all of their AI
outputed code. So almost 100% distrust, but then only half validate. And so the
big question is like why is that the case? Why do we have this big disconnect? And in my mind, one of the biggest reasons for this is out of all
of the teams, the ones that do validate all AI outputed code, they spend on
average 24% of their working hours in a week just looking at AI output. And
trust me, I can tell you from my experience using coding agents for hours and hours a day for the last 2 years,
that becomes a drag. It's a huge mental barrier to just take this bunch of code
that's generated by the agent and review it and then you go and you send off the next request and then you have to review again. It is just it's hard to make
yourself do that all of the time. And the main problem with this is
AI coding agents, they don't do the best job on the first pass, right? Like if you give it a task and you don't give
it guard rails and ways to validate that we'll get into in a little bit. Really even for the top models like GPT 5.3
codecs, Claude Opus 4.6, they only generate correct and secure code 50 to
60% of the time on a first pass. And so we really need to validate, but also
there's just so much that when it comes to our hands, it's not quite right. It's kind of AI slop as it's called in the
industry. And so it's just a hassle to validate everything. So, we need to, but it's really hard to do. So, there's a
lot of solutions for this that I'll get into. But anyway, that's just kind of setting the stage here for the problems that we got right now.
Absolutely. And is that is this a transient problem? You know, the agents are always getting better. The models are getting better. Does this go
away soon or do we have is this a problem we need to solve now and in the future?
Yeah, we definitely need to solve this now and in the future. So, models are getting more and more powerful over time. So, it's true that this issue
becomes less problematic over time. But I don't have the exact study for
this, but it was actually statistically proven that LLMs will always hallucinate. No matter how powerful the
model is, no matter the parameter size, there's always going to be issues that come up because they're prediction
machines. In the end, they can't do true reasoning. They can simulate reasoning with thinking tokens. You've probably
heard of that before, but there's always going to be issues that come up. And honestly, there's kind of been a plateau in power of the LLMs. And so in
the recent like maybe 6 months to a year, it definitely seems like large language models are getting more and more powerful, but it's more the tooling
and the harness around the LLM. Tools like Claude Code that make it seem more
powerful when really the LLM reasoning itself has not been that much better over the past half year. And so that's
what we're going to get into today is talking about these harnesses. what can we wrap around the agent so that we're
not just dealing with what's outputed in the first pass, but we're able to have the agent validate a lot of its own
work. And so, yeah, we'll get into that in a little bit here. I mean, yeah, this is exciting. Let's go for it. So, can you
talk about what does this mean for teams and what does your research say about how we should address this?
Yeah, definitely. Yeah. So when an individual or company wants to start adopting AI coding assistance, there's
always two things that I tell them that they have to address. So there's one big problem and then there's an approach
that I really want to get into. Starting with the problem here, it's that coding agents, they are very prone
to overengineering. And you'll see this with large language models. If you ever have a coding agent run or even just use
an LLM to help you write a blog post or something, they will always lean way too far on the side of being
extremely verbose. And that is a problem for us because that's part of what makes
it so difficult fundamentally to validate AI generated code is there's just always so much of it. And so we
have to sift through so much of this code. That also means there's more code that could be incorrect and have
vulnerabilities, right? There's a larger attack surface overall. And so there's a lot of work that you have to put into
making sure the codebase is simplified. And we'll talk about that too. And there's actually a study that was done.
This is my favorite one I found in my research. It's very thematic to this talk because they used as their tool. But
Carnegie Melon University, they did a study where they took over 800 GitHub repositories
where cursor was the primary driver of the code. So AI code generated and then
they compared it to a batch of GitHub repos that didn't use any AI coding assistant at all. So bunch of AI code, a
bunch of no AI code and they used to determine this metric called
cognitive complexity. And so it's a lot more than just like how many lines are generated. It's not really worth getting
into the weeds here, but it's a pretty elaborate and impressive metric just figuring out like how complex is this
codebase overall. And they found that based on this metric, the code bases that were more AI generated
heavy, they were 40% or more cognitive complex compared to the ones that didn't
use AI coding assistance. That's a huge difference. And so yeah, like AI coding assistants, they love to
implement backward compatibility. They like to have like super complex error handling that you're never going to need. It just becomes a mess to review.
And so yeah, that's the core problem of overengineering. And then the second problem that we need to
address or more of the framework that I want to talk about is how can we get the AI coding assistant to do much
as much of its own validation upfront as possible. So we define here are the
steps you can take in the plan. So before we even write a single line of code, we're telling the agent once you
write the code, here's how you can validate it. It's very akin to test-driven development that we're going to talk about a lot later because before
we even write the code, we're defining the validation steps. And so the goal here is how can we get the agent to
reason about simplifying things and address any kind of vulnerabilities or broken code that we have before
control is passed back to us. And so instead of just reviewing things on the first pass when it's only 50 to 60%
correct, when it comes back to us after it's done a lot of its own validation, then a lot of the cognitive
load is taken away from us so that it's not a huge drag to validate the AI
generated code after every output. I think I think that's the big challenge, isn't it? if you're getting more and more code and it's more and more
complicated and I haven't got another 10 people in my team how on earth do you how can you possibly get
through that without making the human part of the review easier so I mean let's talk about your
framework how how do we do this right yeah so that's the big thing I
want to present here and I actually have a visual for this so my framework that really addresses the overengineering and
all this initial validation is the AI validation pyramid and I'll talk about how these main two
things I'm saying we need to address how the validation pyramid really ties them together. And so what this gives us is a
step-by-step process for both the AI coding assistant and us to review things
over a single feature implementation. And so we can get into what each of these layers really involves, but we
basically start with the lowhanging fruit. We have the agent do things like type checking and linting, then creating
and running unit tests. And again, all of these things we are defining in the plan up front. And so we're being very
specific to the agent once you write the code. This is exactly how you should go through each of these stages. And we
have a lot of code simplification built into this as well if you're doing it right. And so the important thing here is at the top of
the pyramid, we still have us doing the review. I'm not a proponent. A lot of
people will say that like you can get your agents and your system reliable enough where you don't even have to look at the outputed code. I don't think
that's true. I think because like we said LLMs will always hallucinate. It is important for us to review the code at
the end even if we are doing more than just a single pass. But the point is we're at the tip of the iceberg now.
So, a lot of these issues with security and broken code is addressed before it's
our turn to look at things. And we can use AI coding assistance to help our part of the review as well. And so,
we'll get into what all this looks like, but yeah, this is the validation pyramid at a high level. It's
something that I've been spending the last year especially taking a lot of time researching best practices,
implementing this myself, building dozens of applications with it. And this makes a huge difference over just taking
the first pass and dealing with all the AI slop that comes with that. I love the shape of the pyramid.
It sort of feels to me like at the bottom I've got this huge great mess and effort coming to me, but as it gets
further up before it gets to me, you're stripping out that pain. You're stripping out the work before I even get my eyes on it. That's really So let's
So So let's talk type checking and linting. How much is enough? What's
in there? How do you how do you recommend we do that? Yeah. So, linting and type checking, this is the lowest
hanging fruit. And this is actually where it gets the most interesting because you wouldn't think that type-checking and linting is going to
directly lead to more secure and better working code. And it really isn't direct, but there is a
very real correlation here. So, the whole point of type checking and linting is to make our codebase more clean so that both our
coding agents and us can understand and build on top of it better. And this is really important for coding agents
because think about it, LLMs are prediction machines. They see what you already have there, in our case, our
code base, and they predict what comes next. So if we have a very clean code base that it's looking at, then
it's going to mimic that. We have patterns for it to follow already. And a lot of that is what typeing and linting
can help us with because we're looking at any types that aren't respected in our codebase. We're looking for dead code, unused imports. I mean there's
hundreds of things that linting can find and so it helps us build a clean codebase so our coding agent can
continue to maintain the cleanness of the codebase right and like as a part of this validation it will run the linting
on the new features that it built and make sure that it's fixing those things before it passes control back to us so
clean code it's not like we are directly fixing security vulnerabilities or broken code in this first stage of the
validation pyramid but we are preventing it going forward and that equally as important as addressing any current
issues or vulnerabilities that we have. And then so as you go through the linting, it looks like you've then got
sort of two levels of testing. How do you distinguish between which goes into where and
what how do I make sure that I've got a good coherent plan that that's enough but not too much?
Sure. Definitely. Yeah, it's a good question. So for me, unit testing is writing code to test isolated functions
and then the end toend testing is going through entire user journeys as I like to call them. So I'll talk about both
here. So unit testing it's pretty lowhanging fruit just like the type checking and linting. So we take
individual features and we just write a function like we have the coding agent write a function or multiple to try to
hit on different edge cases just to make sure that the core logic of the feature that it implemented is sound. And it's
not like you can catch every security vulnerability with just unit testing, of course, but there are a couple of big ones that you can catch. And so there's
this index of really common vulnerabilities called the CWE is short for common weakness enumeration.
CWE number 20 is bad input validation. And this is one of those things that you
can catch really easily with unit testing, especially those edge cases around like too many characters or not enough characters, things like that that
can really mess up the logic of your application and lead to issues and vulnerabilities. The coding agent does a
really good job at catching those with unit testing. And CWE, by the way, is one of the most common AI generated
vulnerabilities. There are quite a few specifically that happen way more than human generated code because they're
just not thinking about edge cases as much as us. And so that's a lot of what we're trying to catch here in the validation. And so there's not too much
around code simplification in unit tests. It's more about correctness and and the general security of the
application. But then when we get into integration testing, this is where everything really starts to come together. It's less of the lowhanging
fruit compared to type-checking linting and unit testing. But this is where we give the Asian tools to test the
application exactly as a user would. So most applications that I build, they have some sort of front end. And so I
love using browser automation tools like you got the Chrome MCP, the Playright MCP, the Versell agent browser CLI.
There's a million tools out there for this. And so the agent can start your application, visit the site just like a
user would, and then go through all the different ways a user would actually use your application. It can test for
things that might be related to vulnerabilities like it can try SQL injection attacks or cross-sight scripting. like this is its time to dive
into the security of the application as well. And then to take this even further, this is also where I like to
have the agent kind of do some meta reasoning around how much it can maybe simplify the codebase. Trying out
these different features live and then mapping that back to the code and seeing like is there anything here that's unnecessary that can be simplified
because again once control is passed back to me I don't want to review all of this code that's unnecessary. And so
the integration end to end this is like the top part of the pyramid before the control passes back to me. So this is
definitely where I spend most of my time in my planning right defining the steps up front. Here's what you are
going to do to validate things before you write a single line of code it knows that. So once it writes the code it
jumps right into that. And that's kind of the test-driven development approach that we'll talk about more as far as
how much we should have in the plan. It's really just scoped to how
big your feature is. So if you're building a web application, you have an e-commerce platform and you're
implementing a better search functionality. Well, don't try to like do too much regression testing. I mean,
that's important as well, but usually outside of the feature implementation, your validation will just be go to the website, try to
input a filter and make sure that the filter worked and then maybe there's different types of filters.
Like you check all that, you make it very specific to the feature implementation. And then usually I'll do regression testing outside of this
sort of planning, implementing, validating loop that I'm doing. So I just keep it really really focused. Same
thing with the unit testing and the linting and the typeing usually the llinter will run on your entire codebase. So that's more comprehensive.
But for the things that are more involved like the endto-end testing I definitely keep it really scoped into what we're currently working on.
Makes a lot of sense. So okay. So, at this point, so it's entirely possible then you get quite a long way up the pyramid and then your agent, your
reviewer is finding problems and they're going to go make a change and then come back up through the pyramid through the different phases. Is
that right? And then so at this point then so we've got through we're in I sounds like we're in really good shape by that point
and it's now it's time for the human. What part of the of the what's the role of the human in this review then?
Right. Yeah. So well the main thing is even though we are doing a lot more than just a single pass here now that we have
this validation pyramid it can be many passes like you just said John like it finds an issue it addresses that and
then it goes back up the pyramid again. It can do that in an iterative cycle to make sure that everything is working as
perfectly as it can determine but it's never going to be 100% of the way there. So first pass like we were saying
earlier 50 to 60% reliability. With something like the validation pyramid I've seen as high as 90%. But again,
never quite 100. So, it's our job to go especially into the manual testing, thinking about the things that the agent
really isn't thinking about. Business logic is a good example. Like sometimes we just didn't communicate
clearly enough to the agent. How should the filtering work, right? Like, oh, there's this edge case I didn't think
about or there's this specific filter that I forgot to tell it. Often times, it comes back to human error where you're just not specific enough to the
agent for what it needs to build or what it needs to validate. And so actually half the time when I'm doing the
validation myself, it's more figuring out like what did I do wrong in the plan and but it still is a lot of LLM
hallucinations that you have to deal with as well. And so it's a lot of code review plus manually going through the
application kind of going through the same user journeys that the agent did with endto-end testing. And there's two
reasons why this is really not a drag anymore. The first is that the agent going through the first three levels of
the validation pyramid. It's fixing a lot of those initial issues, simplifying the codebase. There's less for us to
review, and there's less slop overall. I'm going to keep using that term because I find it really funny. but
then the other reason this is easier for us is as a part of the validation pyramid for the manual code
reviewing and testing. I use AI coding assistance to help me with this as well. And so, for example, using
to help me like find security issues that I can like take a look at in the codebase myself. Even just using an
AI coding assistant to point out like here are the parts of the codebase that I consider high risk or I think you
should like take a look at because I implemented this feature and I was unsure about this thing. So, you can even ask a coding agent like what part
of the implementation are you not very confident in? And surprisingly, agents do pretty good at reasoning about that.
So, there there's certain meta reasoning that agents can't do well. like thinking about edge cases, but like confidence in
its own application, like I'm usually pretty surprised how accurate it is. And so that kind of helps me focus in on my
code review. That's fascinating. So I I wanted to ask sort of between now and
and a year ago, have you have you seen a significant shift in in how as a
human my review looks? Am I focusing on different things? Is it a different experience?
Yeah. it definitely is especially because of like what I was just saying like using an agent to help you focus
and honestly like I can't also say that the code simplification is 100%
perfect either in the first part of the validation pyramid. So a lot of what's changed with human review now is
figuring out like where did the agent still overengineer like I find myself having to correct the agent so much for
that. As much as I try to like fix my prompts over time to try to prevent it from happening again, it's a lot more of
like let's simplify things. I I feel like before the age of agentic engineering when you would like review
the pull requests of another developer who's obviously not using AI cuz we're talking like you know 2019 or 2020 or
whatever it was more like they would o underengineer right like they wouldn't have the error handling or they would
like skip out on like the logging that they need for the feature. And so I felt like more often than not I was telling
trying to tell people like okay you got to build more here like there's not enough of error handling or logging or whatever. but now like the agents
always have that and so you have to like tone it down a lot where it'll implement the error handling but then it'll like
duplicate the entire API call in your accept statement. Like I've literally seen that happen before. ridiculous
things like that where it's just like way more than you actually need. And so like that that's really the biggest
shift for me is like focusing on the overengineering versus the underengineering.
That's it's really interesting hearing this shift. I mean I was a developer a long time ago. So this is all it's all
fascinating to me to hear what's the same as before and what is wildly different. So speaking of things from
sort of I used to do in the past, manual testing, it's a great way to find
horrible weird bugs. H how does manual testing fit into this into your pyramid?
Yeah. So the manual testing is the top of the pyramid. And honestly, that hasn't changed that much compared to
like before we were using AI coding assistance cuz I still want to like go through the whole like user journey as I
was calling it. And like even though the agent has tested all of that already, I still just kind of want to be the second
eyes on it all. so sometimes if I'm trying to move really quickly, like building more of a proof of concept,
then I'll just kind of trust it, especially because of like browser automation tools, going back to that example, it can take screenshots as it's
doing the testing. So I can like quickly view these screenshots. Like if it shows a screenshot before the filter and then
a screenshot of inputting the text and then a screenshot of the products in the e-commerce catalog like being filtered,
like I mean that's pretty good. I feel like I have to do less manual testing. So maybe as long as there's like proof,
some kind of artifact the agent outputs for its validation, you have to do less. But I still always want to like go
through the flows myself. I think that that's going to lead us nicely into test-driven development. But
before we get into that, when it comes to sort of gooey centric testing, do you find that you have to guide the
agent and say, I I want like you said, I want a screenshot here, I want a screenshot here, and I want you to demonstrate this is the case, or can you
be a little bit looser and it's it can work out sort of the right sort of testing to do,
right? Yeah. So, I try to guide it as much as I can. So this gets back to what I was saying a little bit ago where a
lot of times it's like the business logic or more like the things that the agent can't really think about like how will a user actually be using this
application that's more like you this is a high level architect the person who knows the stakeholders clients customers
whatever like you know how people are actually going to use it and so generally I'm actually pretty not
impressed with how an agent will come up with the end toend testing itself if I'm not giving a lot of guidance and
you're 100% correct John this gets into test room and development because what I'll do here in my plan is I will
define like very meticulously like here's exactly like the buttons you should click like the flow you should go
through once you've implemented the code to make sure it's working. So it's a different kind of test-driven development because from a very
traditional standpoint TDD is you'll like write the unit test or integration tests in code and then they'll fail
initially and then you build the features until they pass. So it's it's similar, but it's less like deterministic. Here's the code that
needs to be passing. And it's more like defining in natural language. Here are the exact steps that you need to take,
but it still is a lot less ambiguous than just defining the feature you want
in natural language and then not saying like what the validation should be at the end. That's what a lot of people do
is they'll they won't focus on like what how do we validate the end result. They'll just focus on like here's the
feature that I want. But getting a lot more like in the weeds and defining that at the end of your plan again before you
get into coding that just goes such a long way because it not only does it help the agent with its testing, but it
also forces you to get really specific. So like a lot of these strategies in the validation pyramid, they're like helping
you as much as they're helping the agent, right? Like our goal is to reduce our cognitive load for the review, forcing us to get really specific
upfront. And so that goes along with what I was saying as well where it's like half the time when there's an issue in the code at the end, it's actually
your fault. Or you could even argue like 100% of the time because it's it's really just like how good is your planning, how good is the validation
that you're defining for the agent because it's your responsibility to I I always say you want to sandwich the
implementation between or like you sandwich it with the planning up front and the validation at the end that
you're very very involved with and then you're just trying to delegate as much of the actual coding to the agent. But
it's not like we're vibe coding here because we're still super involved and taking responsibility for every step of
the way. This is this is so interesting to hear. So when you when you talk about the planning phase, it's really interesting
to me that you don't talk about the spec. It am I right that you you're not writing it perfectly first time the
actual specification. It sounds like it's almost a discussion and and you're evolving what you want a as part of this
planning phase to get to a solid spec. Is that right? Exactly. Yeah. Yep. So, one of the
things I teach all the time in the planning phase, you want to start really unstructured and you evolve to something
more structured because not only does that help you get on the same page with the coding agent, but it's just easier
for you to get over that mental hurdle initially of creating that really detailed spec because there's no pressure on you to do that right away.
Like I'll literally just start by like opening up my coding agent and saying like here's my codebase and I'm
interested in building out this feature. Like what are some ideas you have for this? like a super casual conversation
at first and then what I'll do is I'll have it ask me a bunch of questions about like how I want to implement this.
And so I I like having it guide the conversation because the number one goal with planning is to reduce the number of
assumptions that the coding agent is making. And so if it's asking us all the questions of the assumptions it's
making, we can reduce those. Like I just like knock them out one at a time as it's asking me these questions. And coding agents have gotten really really
good at doing this. It's like Cloud Code for example, it actually has a tool built into the CLI called ask user
question. So it'll like open up this multiplechoice selection here with its ideas and then you can like type a free
form answer if it the multiple choice is like not suitable to you or whatever. I love using that because then by the time
it goes through all the questions and I've explored the codebase and maybe done like web research on best practices for you know like whatever I'm building
then I get into creating the structure plan. And so I use the entire conversation as context and then I'll
have a command that like has my planning template, my spec template. And that includes things like success criteria,
here's the user story, here's how you break things down into a discrete task list. Here's the validation, right?
the end of the plan that we've been talking about a lot. Like that's a part of the the very structured planning process. And then once I have that
single artifact, like this is my markdown document with my spec, then I'll go into a brand new context window
with the agent and just send it that spec because at that point that specification, that single markdown is
all of the context it needs for the implementation. It has all the validation. Here are all the files that
we figured out we need to touch in the codebase from the planning. It's got it all.
That's fantastic. I was going to ask about about context management. Is that is that something you find you have to
really keep an eye on as you go or is it it's part of the way you work is that you're effectively wiping clean the context before and then just using
the plan to kick off work and so you don't need to manage it quite so much. Yeah. So the the two big strategies for
context management is what I just said like you reset the context window between planning and implementation and
then the other honestly probably more important strategy is just to make sure that your spec is very granular. You
dont want to try to do too much at once. It's almost like 90% of the case or almost every single case when someone
has that memory compaction happen in the middle of planning or implementation where the agent runs out of context.
It's because they're just trying to do too much at once. And sometimes it can be really painful to be like, "Okay,
here are the five things I want to do right now. I got to pick one or two, usually just one." And you just want to
try to like do as much as possible. But it just the reliability goes out the roof compared to trying to knock out
five things at once when you just focus on one. You plan for one, you build one, you validate one, and then you go back
and you do it again. That's the PIV loop as I call it. Plan, implement, validate. You're always splitting something up. Like you have a PRD or you
have an epic and jurro, whatever that is. You're splitting it up into individual tasks that are very very focused.
How do you how do you know how big a task should be? The kind of the million-dollar question,
right? Yeah. Now, that's a tough question because it changes a lot over time because it with large language
models having larger and larger context windows, being able to handle more context over time, it really changes
like how far we can push it. And so, this is probably where I don't have like the best single answer. It's like a
muscle that you build over time. You just kind of realize like with the current capabilities of the LLM and the
specific coding agent that I'm using because it also differs between the tool too, like you you build this sense of like here's how much I can build at
once. And so there really isn't a definitive answer because it also entirely depends on the codebase. There's so many variables. It's the LLM,
the codebase and the tool and the specific feature or features that you're looking to build. And so my general
recommendation, I mean coding agents, they allow you to move really fast. And so you can error on the side of trying
to do a little bit too much at first. And then if you see that like what you got back is just really bad. like, "Oh
man, the agent didn't even understand what I was going for here or it skipped the end to end testing cuz the context window got so big it got lost." Like
that's when it's obvious like, "Okay, let me go back and try to do something more focused." So, usually it becomes
really apparent when the control is given back to you. Like at that point of the validation pyramid, if
there's still a lot of issues there, that's when you know that you're trying to do too much at once. And so, you build that muscle pretty quickly.
Great to hear. Yeah. And I think again you're right with the with your pyramid sort of killing off problematic things.
It'll make it really obvious if the code actually is just it's beyond repair. I think one of when sort of friends ask me
how do I get involved in this? It's it's got to be just try it out. So it's great to hear that. I think that that's what you're saying is let's just get in
there. Let's see what happens and is expensive to try again. So if we sort of thinking about sort of
I mean this has been this is a lot of of great information. If you had to pull it back to to a few sort of key
takeaways for people to really sort of have in their heads as they move forwards, what would they be from our discussion?
Yeah. So, I think it really goes back to the two main things that I'm saying you have to address with AI coding
assistance. Number one, you have to solve for the overengineering. That's a lot of you working with the agent in the plan and the validation after making

And in my mind, one of the biggest reasons for this is out of all of the teams, the ones that do validate all AI outputed code, they spend on average 24% of their working hours in a week just looking at AI output. It's a huge mental barrier to just take this bunch of code that's generated by the agent and review it and then you go and you send off the next request and then you have to review again.
